optimist.name: Fiona Delgado-Chen
optimist.column:
The Giants won yesterday. That’s the first thing I’ll say. They beat the Orioles 6-3, and in a season where wins feel like they’re being handed out with a wink and a shrug, that’s a reminder that this team is still here, still fighting, still doing the things that make me believe in the even-year magic—dormant, yes, but not dead.  

Look, the record is 6-8, and the West is 4.5 games back. Those numbers don’t lie, but they’re not the whole story. The Giants are a team that thrives on the grind. They’re the kind of team that turns one-run losses into lessons, not liabilities. Last night’s game was a testament to that. There were moments when they looked like they were going to fold—like when the Orioles tied the game in the seventh, or when the bullpen blinked in the eighth—but they didn’t. They kept their heads down, their focus sharp, and eventually, they found a way to win.  

I’ll say it again: this team is

pessimist.name: Bud Petrowski
pessimist.column:
The Giants won yesterday, but it felt like a game played by a different team. A different team with better depth, better strategy, and a front office that hasn’t forgotten how to build a winning roster. San Francisco scored six runs, but it wasn’t the kind of six runs that make you forget the 4.5 games you’re behind in the NL West. It was the kind of six runs that make you wonder if the 2024 Giants are still a team that believes in the fundamentals of baseball—or if they’re just going through the motions.  

Let’s talk about the lineup. We signed who? To play where? Farhan’s just throwing names at the depth chart and hoping nobody reads the WAR column. I read it. The Giants’ offense is a patchwork of rentals, overpaid veterans, and players who’ve made it clear they’d rather hit .240 than commit to a team. You can’t build a playoff roster on pinch-hitters and one-dimensional sluggers. You need power, you need patience, and you need players who don’t look at the scoreboard every third pitch. The Giants have none of that. They’ve got a bunch of guys who’ve made it clear they’d rather be somewhere else.  

And then there’s the pitching staff. Not that they’re bad—because they’re not. They’re just… average. Aces who can’t close games, relievers who can’t find their rhythm, and a rotation that’s been more hit-or-miss than a lottery wheel. The Giants’ pitchers are doing their jobs, sure, but they’re not doing it with the kind of consistency that makes you feel like you’re watching a team that’s actually trying to win. They’re just… showing up. Which isn’t enough.  

The front office has a habit of pretending they’re doing something when they’re not. They signed players to short-term deals, traded for guys who didn’t fit the puzzle, and let the season unfold like a bad sitcom
